blocks.content: Monday after school. Diego was on his block — sitting on the stoop of his building, backpack still on, eating chips out of a bag like he didn't have anywhere to be.
He'd walked six blocks to warn me. I hadn't gone back to say thank you and that sat wrong.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: I never thanked you. For the thing with the guy.
He shrugged. Moved his backpack so there was room on the stoop. I sat.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Situation seems quiet now. I stayed out of his way.

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: Good. He's not a patient guy but he moves on if you don't give him a reason to stay interested.
He said it like he'd been watching the man for years. Thirteen years old talking like a case study.

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: He's just here. He's always been here. I've known what he does since I was like nine.
He said it without complaint. Not bitter about it, not proud. Just: this is the information the block provided and I have it.

He was still eating chips. His sneaker had a hole starting at the toe. He was thirteen years old and he knew the operating pattern of a drug dealer on his block from years of observation because nobody in his house had ever told him not to pay attention.
I sat with that for a second.
